senses.definition: People who support or follow associationism, the theory that ideas are linked together in the mind.
examples: Early associationists tried to explain thought as a chain of linked ideas.
Modern psychologists still discuss the influence of the British associationists.
The associationists argued that complex beliefs grow from simple sensations.

senses.definition: Groups of people who join together for a shared purpose.
examples: Professional associations often offer training and networking events.
Many cities have cultural associations for different immigrant communities.
Trade associations can influence government policy.

senses.definition: Connections in your mind between one thing and another.
examples: The smell of the sea has strong associations with her childhood.
For many people, the color red has associations with danger.
The brand wants to avoid negative associations with pollution.

senses.definition: The act of linking ideas, things, or people together.
examples: In this study we examine the associations between diet and health.
They found clear associations between stress and sleep problems.
Statistical associations do not always show cause and effect.

senses.definition: Relating to forming mental connections between ideas, events, or experiences.
examples: Children develop language through associative learning.
The experiment tested the mice’s associative memory.
Smell has a powerful associative power with past emotions.

senses.definition: In mathematics or logic, describing an operation where the result does not change when you change how terms are grouped.
examples: Addition of whole numbers is an associative operation.
The teacher explained the associative property of multiplication.
Not all binary operations are associative.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to making mental connections between things.
examples: The words are stored associatively in the learner’s memory.
People tend to think associatively when solving creative tasks.
The system retrieves information associatively rather than by exact address.

senses.definition: In a way that uses the associative property in mathematics or logic.
examples: The function combines the values associatively.
If the operator works associatively, the result is independent of grouping.
The algorithm processes the data associatively to improve speed.

senses.definition: The quality of being able or likely to form connections or relationships.
examples: Human sociability and associativeness support the growth of communities.
Her natural associativeness helped her build a wide professional network.
The study examined children’s associativeness in group play.

senses.definition: Different kinds or degrees of associativity, especially in mathematical or technical contexts.
examples: The paper compared left and right associativities of the operator.
Programming languages may define several associativities for the same symbol.
These structures show different associativities under composition.

senses.definition: The property of an operation where changing how the items are grouped does not change the result.
examples: Associativity is one of the basic properties of addition.
The lack of associativity in the operation makes calculations complex.
The teacher demonstrated associativity using simple numbers.

senses.definition: In computing, the way an operator or memory structure decides which parts to group or access first.
examples: Operator associativity determines how an expression is evaluated.
Cache associativity affects the processor’s performance.
The language uses left-to-right associativity for this operator.

senses.definition: Someone or something that connects or links things together.
examples: In the group, she became the main associator between departments.
The platform acts as an associator of data from many sources.
Good teachers serve as associators of ideas from different subjects.

senses.definition: In mathematics, a term that measures how far an operation is from being associative.
examples: The associator vanishes when the operation is associative.
They computed the associator for the given algebraic structure.
A non-zero associator indicates a non-associative system.

senses.definition: People, tools, or mathematical objects that connect or link things together.
examples: The coordinators acted as associators between the partner organizations.
Modern software tools serve as powerful data associators.
In this theory the associators play a central role.

senses.definition: Relating to forming associations or connections, especially in the mind.
examples: The test measured children’s associatory abilities.
Dreams often follow a loose, associatory logic.
The therapy uses associatory methods to change behavior.

senses.definition: An associate or partner, especially in a French context or older English use.
examples: He served as an associe of the academy for many years.
The firm welcomed a new foreign associe.
Each associe contributed funds to the shared project.

senses.definition: To free someone from blame, guilt, or sin; to pardon or forgive.
examples: The priest claimed he could assoil the penitent of all sins.
No words could assoil him of the guilt he felt.
They prayed that time would assoil their heavy hearts.

senses.definition: To make up for or pay for something wrong; to atone.
examples: He sought to assoil his past mistakes through service.
No sacrifice could assoil their betrayal.
She hoped her kindness would assoil the earlier harsh words.

senses.definition: Freed from blame, guilt, or sin; pardoned or forgiven.
examples: He rose from prayer feeling assoiled of his guilt.
The knight, assoiled of his vows, returned home.
She believed herself assoiled by divine mercy.

senses.definition: Freeing someone from blame, guilt, or sin; pardoning.
examples: The priest was assoiling the sinners one by one.
She imagined a voice assoiling her of every fault.
They begged the king for an assoiling word.

senses.definition: The act of freeing someone from guilt, blame, or sin; absolution.
examples: He sought assoilment for his many crimes.
The ritual promised assoilment of all confessed sins.
True peace came only with assoilment.

senses.definition: Acts of freeing people from guilt, blame, or sin; absolutions.
examples: The priest’s words were many small assoilments over the years.
Legal systems once relied on ritual assoilments.
The story ends with a series of symbolic assoilments.

senses.definition: Frees someone from blame, guilt, or sin; pardons.
examples: He who truly repents, she said, God assoils.
The court assoils the accused of all charges.
No law assoils a man of moral duty.

senses.definition: In Scots law, to clear someone of a legal charge; to acquit.
examples: The court decided to assoilzie the defender.
He hoped the jury would assoilzie him.
The accused was assoilzied of all counts.

senses.definition: Cleared of a legal charge in Scots law; acquitted.
examples: The defender was assoilzied and left the court a free man.
She felt relief when she heard she had been assoilzied.
Several of the accused were assoilzied due to lack of evidence.

senses.definition: Clearing someone of a legal charge in Scots law; acquitting.
examples: The judge was assoilzieing the defender after reviewing the evidence.
By assoilzieing the accused, the court ended the long case.
They argued that assoilzieing him would be unjust.

senses.definition: Clears someone of a legal charge in Scots law; acquits.
examples: If the court assoilzies the defender, he walks free.
The judge assoilzies her of the final charge.
When a jury assoilzies an accused person, the case is over.

senses.definition: To excuse someone, especially from a court appearance; to give a legal excuse.
examples: The lawyer tried to assoin his client from attending.
He was assoined on grounds of illness.
They sought to assoin the witness from giving evidence.

senses.definition: In music, to be performed in a free and unrestricted way, without strict limits.
examples: The singer was instructed to improvise assoluto.
The passage should be played rubato, almost assoluto.
He interpreted the cadenza assoluto, ignoring the strict beat.

senses.definition: The repetition of the same or similar vowel sounds in nearby words, especially in poetry.
examples: The poet used assonance to create a musical effect in the line.
In the phrase 'rise high', the long 'i' sound is an example of assonance.
Songwriters often rely on assonance instead of exact rhyme.

senses.definition: A similarity in sound between words or syllables.
examples: The names share an interesting assonance.
There is a pleasing assonance between the brand names.
He chose the title for its assonance with the main character’s name.

senses.definition: Having noticeable assonance; marked by repeated vowel sounds.
examples: The assonanced lines gave the poem a gentle rhythm.
An assonanced phrase can be easy to remember.
He favored richly assonanced song lyrics.

senses.definition: Repeated vowel sounds or examples of assonance in language.
examples: The poem is full of soft assonances.
She pointed out several assonances in the passage.
The writer uses assonances and internal rhyme to build rhythm.

senses.definition: Having the same or similar vowel sounds, especially in stressed syllables.
examples: The assonant words tied the stanza together.
In this line, 'stone' and 'road' are assonant.
Assonant pairs can create a strong musical effect.

senses.definition: Relating to or using assonance.
examples: The poem’s assonantal structure is very clear.
She analyzed the assonantal effects in the lyrics.
The passage is rich in assonantal patterns.

senses.definition: Using or showing assonance, especially in poetry.
examples: The assonantic line echoes with long 'o' sounds.
Critics praised the assonantic richness of the text.
He preferred an assonantic style to strict rhyme.

senses.definition: With similar vowel sounds; in a way that shows assonance.
examples: The names are assonantly linked.
The words fall assonantly across the line.
The poet writes assonantly to create a haunting mood.

senses.definition: Words or sounds that share similar vowel sounds.
examples: The poet grouped several assonants together for effect.
These assonants give the line a strong rhythm.
She listed the main assonants in the stanza.

senses.definition: To have similar vowel sounds or to form assonance.
examples: The two lines assonate but do not rhyme fully.
These words assonate nicely in the middle of the phrase.
He chose names that assonate with one another.

senses.definition: Had similar vowel sounds or formed assonance.
examples: The closing words assonated with those in the opening line.
Names that assonated were chosen for the characters.
Several stressed syllables assonated in the middle of the stanza.

senses.definition: Has similar vowel sounds or forms assonance.
examples: The phrase assonates with the one before it.
This title assonates nicely with the author’s name.
The line assonates but does not rhyme exactly.

senses.definition: Having or producing similar vowel sounds, creating assonance.
examples: Assonating phrases run through the poem.
He kept repeating assonating patterns in his lyrics.
The assonating vowels give the speech a chant-like quality.

senses.definition: A very rare variant or misspelling related to assonance.
examples: The term assonia appears only in a few old texts.
Some dictionaries list assonia as an obsolete form.
She confused assonia with the more common assonance.

senses.definition: A very rare and unclear word, possibly a variant or error in older texts.
examples: The manuscript contains the obscure word assoria.
Scholars are unsure what assoria originally meant.
He treated assoria as a textual mistake.

senses.definition: To put things into different groups or types.
examples: Volunteers helped to assort the donated clothes by size.
The samples were carefully assorted before testing.
Please assort the documents into three piles.

senses.definition: To match or go well together.
examples: The colors assort nicely in this design.
Their tastes do not assort well at all.
The partners assort perfectly in terms of skills.

senses.definition: Showing a tendency to pair or group with similar individuals.
examples: Humans often show assortative mating based on education.
The study found assortative patterns in friendship networks.
Assortative behavior can increase social inequality.

senses.definition: In a way that tends to pair or group similar individuals together.
examples: People tend to marry assortatively with respect to income.
The population is structured assortatively by age.
The data show that students sort themselves assortatively by ability.

senses.definition: Of different types mixed together.
examples: The box contains assorted chocolates.
They served a plate of assorted cheeses.
The store sells assorted household items.

senses.definition: Carefully chosen and arranged into a useful group.
examples: The teacher handed out an assorted set of reading materials.
The kit includes an assorted selection of tools.
We received an assorted bundle of marketing samples.

senses.definition: The quality of having different kinds or types mixed together.
examples: The assortedness of the group made discussion lively.
High assortedness in a sample can complicate analysis.
They enjoyed the sheer assortedness of the festival events.

senses.definition: Different kinds or degrees of assortedness.
examples: The samples showed various assortednesses in their contents.
We compared the assortednesses of the two product lines.
Different assortednesses can appeal to different customers.

senses.definition: A person or machine that separates things into groups or types.
examples: The company hired an assorter to organize returned goods.
An automatic assorter divides the mail by postcode.
Each line has a dedicated assorter for quality control.

senses.definition: People or machines that separate things into groups or types.
examples: Several assorters work on the packaging line.
The factory installed new electronic assorters.
Human assorters check what the machines miss.

senses.definition: Separating things into different groups or types.
examples: Workers are assorting the parcels by destination.
She spent the afternoon assorting her clothes.
The system is busy assorting incoming data.

senses.definition: Showing sorting or grouping by type, sometimes used instead of 'assortative'.
examples: The assortive process separated the materials by weight.
Some researchers use the term 'assortive mixing' in networks.
Assortive mechanisms can shape how people choose partners.

senses.definition: A group of different things that are put together.
examples: They served an assortment of sandwiches and salads.
The shop sells a wide assortment of souvenirs.
The box contained a random assortment of tools.

senses.definition: The act or process of separating things into groups or types.
examples: The assortment of goods takes place in the warehouse.
Careful assortment of applicants is important for the program.
The machine handles the assortment of recyclable materials.

senses.definition: Groups of different things that are put together.
examples: The bakery offers several assortments of cookies.
Gift shops sell seasonal assortments.
Supermarkets prepare holiday assortments for customers.

senses.definition: Separates things into different groups or types.
examples: The system assort s incoming emails by priority.
She assort s the beads by color.
The machine assort s the fruit by size.

senses.definition: Matches or goes well together.
examples: The furniture assort s well with the curtains.
Her calm assorts with his more active nature.
The flavors assort nicely in this dish.
